sentences.text: I don't know, actually.
But I suppose so that the guards could see whether anybody was trying to escape or to count us.
They were forever counting us in Theresienstadt-- in Westerbork, excuse me.
No.
Not in Westerbork.
There was no selection.
We were all herded directly into the camp.
The gate consisted essentially of just wood  and barbed wire.
And it was open.
And we were all herded through.
And then we stood in line.
In Westerbork, we always stood in line.
There was nothing you could do where you didn't stand in line.
In this case, it was for the registration, so-called, and the medical examination, so-called.
Because the registration was simply looking at your papers and whether you had an S on your papers, which stood for the German word Strafe, punishment.
And then you went into a different barracks.
Or to see whether the family was really all together [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] more than really anything else.
And to make a note of your transport number, which became an ID number.
And I supposed to keep, as Germans were want to do in those days, a card catalog of whoever was there.
The medical examination, all I remember of it is we had to strip naked.
Now, whether that was because they wanted to see that we were-- whether we were hiding something, I don't know.
I don't offhand remember actually being examined, but we might have been.

That I really-- the recall doesn't go that far, partly because as we went very slowly into this registration building-- it was just a wooden barracks, as they all were, most of them were in Westerbork-- my mother made me sit down on a chair a little off to the side and said, now you stay there, and we'll come back for you.
The only problem was that everybody went into this big barracks, and I didn't see anybody come out.
So I got scared, and, of course, I started crying.
I was age 10 by then.
And they had disappeared in this barracks.
And I heard noises in there.
And I heard voices, but nobody was coming out.
What I didn't know, of course, was that they came out on the other end.
So some adult-- some man whom I didn't know and who didn't know me-- finally saw me, wanted to know what was happening, which I told him.
And he then took me in and found my mother for me.
I do not know how.
And my mother was most upset.
Why are you crying?
We told you we would come back for you.
And all I could say was well, I didn't know whether you were ever coming back.
So that was the beginning of Westerbork.
And during that registration, we were assigned to a barracks.
They were all very long.
They looked like, oh, temporary buildings do here.
Made of wood, very long, and very low, with really one great big room on either side of the entrance which was in the middle, with-- together with a little hallway.
And the men slept on the one side.
The women slept on the other side.
About 300 people to a room, in bunk beds, three high, made of metal.
And we children learned very quickly to climb up like little monkeys.
For the adults, it took a little longer, needless to say.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The problem with Westerbork as a location is that it is quite literally in the middle of nowhere.
There's nothing around there, then or now.
It's a sort of a marshy ground area.
It's out in the country.
It's a lot of heaths, and that's it.
Holland is totally flat.
It has no mountains.
So you could see in all directions.
And it wasn't built as a concentration camp, but in '41 or '42-- I don't remember offhand-- the Nazis took it over and made it into one.
And as such, it was a good location because you really couldn't go anywhere.
Sure, you could flee, but where would you go?
Anything that's around there is little villages, even now, that have maybe a church, a cafe, and three houses, and with luck, a school.
I mean, you'd stand out as a stranger immediately.
Those of us who came from Germany, especially the adults, had an accent, of course, in Dutch.
And unless the villagers were likely to hide you, you'd had it.
The other disadvantage was because of that kind of terrain and it's being so open, the wind always blew and blows.
It still does.
Of course, that hasn't changed.
And there were a lot of flies.
There were sand fleas and whatever-- whatever other insects may be flying around on that kind of terrain.
And, of course, they all came into the camp, in addition to which there were fleas located originally in the straw sacks which we used as mattresses on those bunk beds.
Those 300 people in one room came from everywhere.
It went all the way from what used to be called lower, lower class to really high up higher class kind of people.
I hate to use those terms.
But at that time they made sense.
Not everybody had the same hygiene upbringing.
Some people washed every day.
Some people washed once a month.
I mean, these things happen.
So fleas had a good time.
We did not, but they did.
One of the things we all had to learn was catch and kill, naturally.
Then they got entangled in the blankets.
We did not have sheets.
We had the-- the straw mattress, a straw pillow, no sheets, and the blankets which we'd brought from home.
And the fleas get entangled in those European wool blankets, and they're almost impossible to get out.
So that was one of the relatively minor problems.

At the end of each barracks, on the short end, was a washroom, one for the men, one for the women, with, running down the middle, a metal long sink, all over the whole length of the washroom, with faucets about, oh, I guess every three feet.
Yeah, about a meter between them.
Cold water only.
And we were supposed to use that for washing ourselves, for washing our clothes, for washing anything and everything that needed washing.
And somewhere in a corner was a toilet, either a flush toilet, which mostly didn't work, or what we would now call a chemical toilet-- I don't know what they were called then -- for the 300 people.
And again, as I said earlier, standing in line was normal for that.
They also had outhouses, again, with standing in line.
Equally, if not more disagreeable.
The adults had to work, whatever work was assigned to them.
For the children, there was literally nothing.
We didn't work.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There was nothing to do.
There was no school.
There were no books.
There were no toys.
There was nothing except what we had brought, like my doll.
So mostly we just hung around and got into trouble in one way or another.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The work for the adults was not necessarily according to their profession.
My father, like my grandfather, was a pharmacist, yet in Westerbork, he had to work in what was then called the metal industry.
Had great big pipes, like sewer pipes, that had to be flattened-- I do not know what for-- with a sledgehammer.
And that's what he did, all day long.
And I don't know how well he did at it.
I do know that we all wore the Dutch wooden shoes.
They had been given us when we first came, in exchange for our own shoes.
And actually, they're quite comfortable once you get used to them.
But they are made out of one piece of wood.
And as a result, the piece that's above your foot doesn't give when you walk.
The whole shoe comes up.
So in the beginning, you're likely to get sores or blisters or whatever.
And until the end of her life, my mother had bony outgrowth on top of her feet because of the wooden shoes.
My father invariably got sores on his feet and open-- and open spots.
I got used to them.
I liked them.
I don't think I can still walk in them.
But I have a pair.
We wore our own clothes, in as far as we didn't grow out of them.
Obviously, the adult didn't grow out of them, but the children grew, although not much.
So we wore our own clothes until they were so much too small that we just had to get something else.
Food was brought to us.
Some inmates were assigned to the kitchen and either two or three times a day came by into the barracks with a little cart on which were big containers with the appropriate soup or whatever.
I don't really remember any soup.
I do remember the bread, black in color.
I have no idea what it was made of.
It wasn't totally bread.
It's probably better not to know.
And something on top, on your portion, which was for a limited time, like one day or two days.
On top of that portion sat a little square of neither butter nor margarine.
I do not know what it was, but it was called sana.
I have no idea what it was.
Occasionally there was a little bit of sugar or a little bit of jam for whatever reason.
The adults got coffee.
That means a black liquid.
The children, I don't know.
We probably drank water.
How healthy the water was, I don't know either.
But we drank it.
For cooked food we got presumably soup, some potatoes.
Something must have been made of cabbages because the food was trucked in.
And my father told me the story that after about a month or six weeks or so, I had learned very quickly that we didn't get very much to eat.
So I stole a cabbage from the back of the truck.
Highly dangerous, of course, but I managed.
Brought it home, very proud, and my father said, my daughter doesn't steal.
Take it back.
Now he must not have been very hungry yet, at that time, because later on in Theresienstadt he no longer protested.
[CHUCKLES] But at that time, he still protested.
So they must have used those cabbages for something.
Six months.
From June 20 of '43 till January 18 '44.
Then we were transported on, deported again to Theresienstadt.
In Czech, it's called 'Ter-ah-zeen' or 'Ter-az-in'.
I'm not sure how it's pronounced.
That trip took two days, because the arrival date was January 20th.
And I again remember it as cattle cars, though I can't be sure.
Somehow, those two trips have fused.
They've-- they've all become one.
And again, we went through something that there had the nickname schleuse.
That means sluice or what ships go through, a weir or something like that, where you-- where you heighten and lower the water.
That kind of thing.
Oh, schleuse.
That [INAUDIBLE].
Yeah, I can't remember the word.
What it was in Theresienstadt, it was a nice euphemism for official stealing.
Officially, it was, again, registration and medical examination.
In actual fact, we got stripped naked again.
And by the time you got your luggage back, which went a slightly different way, half of it had disappeared.
Half of your clothes had disappeared.
Whatever foodstuffs you had with you had also disappeared.
And that was the end you'd seen of it.
That was it.
Again, we got assigned to barracks.
But Theresienstadt is-- was and is a different type of a place.
Westerbork was meant as a temporary place, even though there were people who spent the whole war there.
Theresienstadt was originally built as a fortified town for soldiers, as a sort of a-- a guard in case what was then Czechoslovakia got attacked.
It wasn't ever used for that.
And it was built in I think, the late 1780's.
But nothing changed between then and the time we got there.
In 1941, the Germans kicked out all the original inhabitants and sent in the first prisoners, essentially, to ready what became a concentration camp.
It's often called a ghetto.
It wasn't.
And to ready it meant to put up bunk beds and, I suppose, subdivide rooms, and so on.
What hadn't changed was the washrooms, which were much like Westerbork.
Any kind of hygienic appliances, they had been built, I suppose, to the latest standards in the 1780s, but by 1940 it was slightly different.
The wash rooms did have faucets.
Very often they didn't work.
And you had to go and get water from the pump.
The barracks were stone barracks built in a square with a courtyard in the middle.
And in the middle of that courtyard was a hand pump, in most barracks.
And that's where I remember getting our water.
You went down with a pail, you pumped the water, and then you lugged your pail back up to where you lived.
And again, we had big rooms for about 50 people, I guess, then, with bunk beds, three high again, but now made of wood.
And that became important, eventually.
There were some smaller rooms, probably originally officer's rooms, but now also shared.
There was not ever, I think, one person per room.
Later, we had one of those small rooms, and we shared it.
There were five people, five of us in that room, which was meant for one person.
Theresienstadt had originally being built for 7,000 people, half soldiers and half civilians, roughly.
By the time we got there, it held mostly 60,000 people.
So was a little overcrowded.
As a result, the only place we had to put anything, as in Westerbork, but with less space, was our bed.
Straw mattresses and pillows again.
No sheets again.
Our own blankets again.
Nothing to hang anything on.
No nails or hooks.
No closets.
So everything was either put on your bed or under your mattress or in some way stowed away under your pillow.
And you could only hope it wasn't stolen when you came back.
Was there much stealing?
Yes, in both camps.
There was a lot of stealing.
Partly food, but also other things because you could then barter.
As I bartered my doll clothes, you could also barter other things for food or wood, if your room happened to have a little stove or something like that, or cigarettes which then bought other things.
So anything was useful.
Well, let's see, I got there in January of '44.
The official visit, I think, was in June of that year.
I don't remember the visit.
I just know about it.
They came with a very small committee.
And they were shown around.
But they were literally not taken by the hand, but they might as well have been, and showed around.
The Nazis only showed them what the Nazis wanted them to see, however.
And by then, the town had been what they nowadays call beautified.
Literally, facades had been painted.
Street signs had been put up.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] All the ground floor apartments or rooms had been nicely furnished.
Nobody was allowed to live in them, of course.
Anything that was originally a store in the olden days had been filled with clothing or food or whatever.
We couldn't buy it, but that's beside the point.
And so this is what the Red Cross saw.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS]
No, there was no stadium.
But football, European football, soccer, was played.
And there again, that was a forced activity.

Once the beautification was done, the Germans decided to use that beautification to make a propaganda movie, from the German view point that how-- to show mostly the Germans, but also some others, how beautiful Theresienstadt was and how wonderful it was for us Jews to live there and what a nice life we had.
Whereas the poor Germans had to work hard and didn't have enough to eat and so on.
So they used all these painted facades and so on.
And in addition to that, they filmed a number of things.
They filmed the concert.
They filmed the lecture.
And there were those, in fact, even before.
There were a lot of lectures.
Leo Baeck, the rabbi, had a lot of lectures.
The one that is filmed in the film is Otto Ungar, whose daughter is still alive in Czechoslovakia, I think, today.
And he was a professor of something at the university.
So he gave a number of lectures.
And anybody who had any kind of specialty gave lectures, and people came.
Yes, the inmates.
Yes.
The inmates gave lectures to the inmates to pass the time.
Oh, it's a total hoax.
There is nothing real about it whatsoever.
It is true that lectures took place.
But the lecture that is filmed was a forced lecture.
That is to say, people were told, you go there at that time, or else.
The foot-- the soccer game.
It was not in a stadium.
It was in the courtyard of one of the barracks.

And the courtyard, it was small enough that the-- the two [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] groups that played, instead of having 11 players had only seven, because the courtyard couldn't hold anymore than that.
And again, you see in the movie, that part of still-- of-- that part of it still exists.
You see a lot of people, audience.
And again, they were forced to go there.
There was another segment that no longer exists about a diving competition in a swimming pool.
A, there was no swimming pool.
B, it was done in the river.
C, what they didn't show was the Nazis in boats on either side with their guns trained on the divers.
So it was just totally hoax.
They built a cafe.
We never were supposed to go in there.
And after the movie was done, it was taken away again.
But there is-- there was a segment showing inmates in the cafe.
So it's-- nothing that is shown in there was really true.
Were you in any part of this movie?
I'm in the last part of it, yes.
The last part that exists.
There's very little of it left.
But in one of the very last pictures--
In one of the last pictures, there are a number of women sitting around a table, and a couple of little girls on a bench before the table, but with their backs towards the table.
The women are doing their hair or just sitting.
And I recognized myself when I saw it.
I have clothes on that didn't belong to me.
That was another hoax.
We were given clothes.
Makeup and hairdressing was done by professionals.
They were supposed to look healthy.
Not-- not there, I don't think.
But I had a blue skirt and a white blouse, neither of which I owned.
And my mother is sitting in the background on the other side of the table, looking rather glum on the whole.
So.
Actually, I don't think it was ever used.
But it was meant to be used in Germany, I suppose to stir up more Jew hatred.
Because if the Jews had it so good, and the Germans had it so poor, then obviously something was wrong, from their point of view.
I know the director, yes.
It was ordered by [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] an officer, a Nazi officer by the name of Gunter, I think that-- I think that's his last name, Hans Gunter.
In the Martyrs Memorial, amongst other places.
Also in the Washington Holocaust Museum.
Also in the Wiesenthal Museum.
There are actually a number of prints around.
There is one print in England, in the War Memorial Museum, or some such thing.
They have one print in Israel.
To the places I just mentioned.
To the Martyrs Memorial, the Wiesenthal Center, the Washington Holocaust Museum, to the-- what I call the Theresienstadt Kibbutz in Israel, [INAUDIBLE].
The official name is Givat Chaym Ichud.
And it's somewhere between Tel Aviv and-- and Haifa, about midway.
And I call it that because about, I think, three quarters of the inhabitants are Theresienstadt survivors.
And they have a smallish museum on Theresienstadt.
They also have copies of all the registration cards that were in the Theresienstadt card catalog.
Now computerized, but they have the-- the copies, and a lot of information in general.
Mostly the overcrowding, the noise, the enormous number of people.
The eternal noise, the fact that nobody had any privacy.
There were no curtains or anything between the beds, in Westerbork either.
The standing in line, again, for everything, including food.
And the food itself, which of course, was terrible.
Apart from not being enough, it was awful.
Again, we had potato soup with sometimes a little bit of peel in it.
It looked-- and I suppose tasted-- like dish water.
We had a barley soup that tasted and looked much alike.
We had some sort of a cereal that looked yellowish-green, and which at the time I liked.
The consistency was kind of like oatmeal, but it wasn't oatmeal.
We had bread, again black.
And again, I don't know what was in it, except it made a noise between your teeth as though you were eating sand.
I'm not saying sand was in it.
It just made that noise.
And of course, none of-- none of it was enough.
But in this case, we had to go get it rather than it being brought to the barracks.
I suppose partly because of so many people being there.
No.
Theresienstadt was the portal to Auschwitz, Sobibor, any of the other camps.
Either the-- either work camps or death camps, whichever.
And again, I think it went random.
I don't know how they figured out what transport went where.
At the end of this propaganda movie-- it was finished in September of '44-- there were several of what are now called autumn transports, or fall transports.
And most of the people who had worked-- the inmates were the actors, of course.
So was the director, for that matter, an inmate.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] They were all transported.
Every single one of them, they were transported to Auschwitz, and they were gassed there.

That includes the director and script writer, who was Kurt Gerron, a well-known actor and director in Germany, before the war, who had gone to Holland, had gone to Westerbork, where we knew him, my family and I.
Had then gone to Theresienstadt, was forced to make that movie.
And was for his pains transported, deported to Auschwitz and was killed there.
Another person that worked with that movie but who survived is a Dutch artist, Yos Speer.
He is dead by now, but his sons-- his two sons live in the States, and his daughter lives in Canada.
And he was forced as well to work on it.
He was a-- a graphic artist, painting, drawing, and so on.
And he was forced to make drawings for the children's room, which didn't exist, of course, which was filmed, and did that.
Well, he couldn't refuse obviously, not and live.
But it was all forced work, as was everything else we did.
Any kind of work assigned to the adults was of course forced.
The children were forced to work as of age 10.
Yes.
I had just turned 10 when we went to Westerbork.
By this time, I was 10 and 1/2.
And I was officially assigned to be a message carrier-- then they called it an order nonce-- to carry written or oral messages from my base to wherever else, wherever they sent me.
But on several occasions, I had a different kind of a job.
They called the children to the crematorium.
Both places, both camps had a crematorium.
Not because they were death camps, but because they had to dispose of the dead bodies.
Theresienstadt could not have a cemetery because the ground was too damp, and water seeped in.
So they cremated the bodies.
And us children were sent there in a sort of a fireman's line, the old fashioned fireman's line, to give boxes from one child to another.
The boxes contained the ashes of the dead.
And they were loaded on trucks and taken away.
I didn't know then what happened to them.
I found out much later through reading that those ashes were dumped in the river.
And that was one of my jobs.
To pass on the boxes from one child to the next.
So I received from one child and gave to the next child.
By that time, I didn't feel anymore.
And it was perfectly normal.
It felt good, in a way, because we got 'paid,' in quotes, for that.
We got an extra little tiny piece of sausage.
When you're that hungry, even that little bit feels good.
